page.projects.title: Selected Work
project.plugin-system.title: Job Application Tracker — Chrome Extension
project.plugin-system.insight: 1-click job logging from LinkedIn, Indeed, and Totaljobs. The extension reads the page automatically; Notion stores the record — no copy-pasting, no form-filling required.
project.plugin-system.problem: Tracking job applications manually is tedious and error-prone. Switching between a job board, a spreadsheet, and notes wastes several minutes per application — and details get lost. The goal was to reduce the friction to zero: one click from any listing page and the application is logged.
project.plugin-system.method: Built a Chrome Extension (Manifest V3) with per-site content scripts that extract job title and company directly from the DOM on LinkedIn, Indeed, and Totaljobs. Clicking the popup pre-fills the fields — the user confirms, selects a status, adds optional notes, and clicks Log. A single API call writes the record to Notion. Credentials are stored in Chrome Storage Local so they never need re-entering. For unsupported job boards, an optional Claude fallback can parse the page if an Anthropic API key is configured.
project.plugin-system.finding: End-to-end logging time dropped from ~2 minutes to under 5 seconds. Claude's structured-output mode proved reliable even on highly variable job-board HTML — no custom parsers per site were needed. The Notion integration made it easy to add custom fields (priority, cover-letter status) without touching the extension code.
project.plugin-system.impact: The extension is installable from source in under two minutes and works on all major job boards (LinkedIn, Indeed, Glassdoor, Lever, Greenhouse). The architecture is intentionally minimal — swapping Notion for another storage target requires changing only one module.
plugin.hook: Every job application involves the same 2-minute ritual: copy the title, open a spreadsheet, paste the details, close the tab — and lose your place. A Chrome Extension collapses that into one click without ever leaving the listing.
plugin.metric.0.label: Log from any job board
plugin.metric.1.label: Time to log
plugin.metric.2.label: Context switches required
plugin.metric.2.sub: Tool lives where the intent is
plugin.metric.3.label: Notion credentials
plugin.situation.label: The Problem
plugin.situation.body: The manual tracking ritual: find a role, feel interested, open a new tab, try to recall the job title and company, paste everything into a spreadsheet, close the tab, lose your place on the job board. At scale this either gets skipped — and you lose track of where you applied — or it's painful enough that you stop applying systematically. The cost of recording a job was high enough to change behaviour.
plugin.insight.label: The Insight — Capture at the Moment of Intent
plugin.insight.body: The problem wasn't that tracking tools were bad. It was that they all asked you to go somewhere else to use them. Any context switch — even opening a new tab — competes with the momentum of job searching. The insight: the capture tool has to live exactly where the intent is. On the listing page, at the moment you decide 'I want to track this.'
plugin.insight.quote: The best time to log a job application is while you're reading it. A Chrome Extension is the only tool that's already there.
plugin.decisions.label: Key Decisions
plugin.decision.0.what: Chrome Extension, not a web app
plugin.decision.0.why: A web app requires the user to open it, paste the job title, and fill in fields manually — adding 2+ minutes and a context switch. An Extension lives in the browser and runs on the listing page itself. Zero navigation, zero copy-pasting.
plugin.decision.1.why: Manual form fields require the user to type the job title and company — which still means reading the page and re-entering what's already there. Content scripts read the DOM directly and pre-fill the fields. The user confirms, not types. That's the difference between a utility and a friction-free tool.
plugin.decision.2.what: Chrome Storage Sync for API persistence
plugin.decision.2.why: The first version required entering the API key on every click. I was used to that — I'm a developer. A real user isn't. Fixing it with Chrome Storage Sync took 20 minutes of code, but it was the most important change: you enter your key once and never see it again.
plugin.reflection.label: What I'd Do Differently
plugin.reflection.body: The first version I shipped was a developer experience, not a user experience. I had to enter my API key every single click — because I built it for myself and I'm used to that kind of friction. The fix was simple, but the lesson wasn't: when you're your own first user, you have blind spots about what 'normal' friction looks like. The next step is testing it with someone who has never touched an API key.
plugin.feature.0.title: Automatic DOM extraction
plugin.feature.0.body: Per-site content scripts read job title and company directly from the page on LinkedIn, Indeed, and Totaljobs. No copy-pasting, no manual entry. For unsupported sites, an optional Claude fallback can extract details if an Anthropic API key is configured.
plugin.feature.1.title: One-click logging
plugin.feature.1.body: A single click in the popup confirms the extracted fields and writes the record to Notion. The whole flow takes under five seconds from any job listing page.
plugin.feature.2.title: Notion database integration
plugin.feature.2.body: Each application becomes a rich Notion page with structured properties. Add custom fields — priority, cover-letter status, follow-up date — without changing the extension.
plugin.feature.3.title: Works everywhere
plugin.feature.3.body: The content script runs on all job boards. LinkedIn, Indeed, Glassdoor, Lever, Greenhouse — if the role is on a webpage, the extension can log it.

project.airbnb-london.title: London Airbnb Market Analysis
project.airbnb-london.insight: 60K+ listings across 33 neighbourhoods — spatial pricing, seasonal demand, and host concentration explored through R and Tableau.
project.airbnb-london.problem: Inside Airbnb publishes rich listing and calendar data for London, but raw data alone hides the spatial and seasonal patterns that matter to hosts, guests, and policymakers. The goal was to turn three messy CSVs into a coherent market picture.
project.airbnb-london.method: Cleaned and reshaped the listings, calendar, and reviews datasets in R — handling missing prices, normalising room types, and computing monthly occupancy proxies from calendar availability. Built four analytical layers: spatial price mapping by neighbourhood, seasonal demand trends, supply vs. occupancy dynamics, and top-10 host concentration. Visualised in a single multi-sheet Tableau dashboard.
project.airbnb-london.finding: Inner-east and central neighbourhoods command a consistent price premium. Occupancy peaks sharply in summer and around major events. A small cluster of multi-listing hosts accounts for a disproportionate share of supply — a pattern typical of platform economies.
project.airbnb-london.impact: The dashboard lets any stakeholder slice by neighbourhood, room type, and month in real time. Hosts can benchmark their pricing; analysts can identify demand windows; the Tableau workbook is fully reproducible from the Inside Airbnb source data.
olist.agent.intro: A conversational Claude-powered agent that runs the full analytical pipeline on demand. Ask it anything about churn, segments, or revenue — it selects and chains the right tools automatically.
olist.tool.0.title: Load Data
olist.tool.0.body: Merges 5 Olist CSVs (~100K transactions) into a single analytical dataframe with parsed timestamps and derived columns.
olist.tool.1.title: RFM Segmentation
olist.tool.1.body: Scores every customer on Recency, Frequency, and Monetary value, then assigns one of 6 business segments — Champions through Lost.
olist.tool.2.title: K-Means Clustering
olist.tool.2.body: Fits K-Means on log-scaled RFM features; validates k with silhouette score and auto-labels clusters (High-Value, Dormant, Recent, Mid-Tier).
olist.tool.3.title: Churn Prediction
olist.tool.3.body: Trains a Random Forest on 7 features; returns ROC-AUC, precision/recall, feature importances, and R$ revenue at risk from high-probability churners.
olist.tool.4.title: Cohort Retention
olist.tool.4.body: Builds a full cohort retention matrix and surfaces month-2 and month-3 retention rates with a plain-language intervention recommendation.
olist.tool.5.title: Pareto Analysis
olist.tool.5.body: Quantifies revenue concentration: what share do the top 10% and 20% of customers contribute, and what is the median vs. mean spend.
olist.tool.6.title: A/B Test Design
olist.tool.6.body: Computes required sample size and statistical power for a retention experiment given baseline rate, expected lift, α, and β.
project.job-agent.title: AI Job Application Agent
project.job-agent.insight: Paste a job URL or description → get a match score, skill gap analysis, tailored cover letter, and ATS keyword recommendations in ~20 seconds. Powered by Claude, deployable as a CLI, REPL, or Feishu chatbot.
project.job-agent.problem: Preparing a quality job application takes 30+ minutes per role: reading the JD, mentally matching skills, drafting a cover letter, and guessing which keywords an ATS will look for. At scale this is unsustainable — candidates either rush applications or apply to fewer roles than they should.
project.job-agent.method: Built a 4-layer Python agent: an orchestrator that parses user intent (URL, raw text, or command), a tools layer that fetches and cleans JD HTML via httpx + BeautifulSoup, a Claude reasoning layer that scores skill match (0–100), identifies gaps, generates a role-specific cover letter using keyword-frequency template selection, and recommends ATS keywords — all in a single API call with delimiter-based dual-format output (Markdown + JSON). A Feishu bot channel uses Flask + background threading to stay within the platform's 3-second webhook timeout.
project.job-agent.finding: Keyword-frequency template selection matched the correct role type in 100% of test cases with zero ML overhead. Delimiter-based prompt engineering (===MARKDOWN_START=== / ===JSON_START===) ensured 100% parseable LLM output. Honest match scoring surfaced real gaps — a 28% score correctly flagged missing FX/trading domain knowledge that would have been missed in a manual review.
project.job-agent.impact: Application prep dropped from 30+ minutes to under 30 seconds. The modular architecture passes the 'swap test' — replace Claude with any LLM and the system still works. Three deployment interfaces (CLI, interactive REPL, Feishu chatbot) make it usable for solo job searches or team-wide deployment.
jobagent.hook: Job searching has a hidden tax: 3 minutes reading a JD, 20 minutes writing a cover letter — before you even know if you're a real fit. I built a tool to collapse that cost to 20 seconds.
jobagent.metric.0.label: JD to full assessment
jobagent.metric.1.label: Outputs per JD
jobagent.metric.1.sub: Match score · Cover letter · Interview prep
jobagent.metric.2.label: Job markets designed for
jobagent.metric.3.label: Interview prep feature
jobagent.situation.label: The Problem
jobagent.situation.body: The frustration isn't rejection — it's the time invested before finding out. My job search kept following the same cycle: read a JD carefully, feel excited about the role, start drafting a cover letter — then discover 3 missing skills and start over. At scale, this either burns candidates out or pushes them toward scattershot, low-effort applications. Neither outcome serves anyone.
jobagent.insight.label: The Insight — What JDs Don't Tell You
jobagent.insight.body: JDs are written by HR. Interviews are run by hiring managers. What gets listed isn't always what gets tested. Even when I understood a role's requirements, I still didn't know what the interviewer actually cared about — the unstated priorities behind the bullet points. This gap became the interview prep feature: not just gap analysis against the JD, but a layer that surfaces what's likely to come up in the room.
jobagent.insight.quote: Understanding a JD tells you what they want. The interview prep feature tries to tell you what they'll ask.
jobagent.decisions.label: Key Decisions
jobagent.decision.0.why: Chinese professionals manage job searches through work tools, not personal apps. Meeting users where they already work means zero onboarding friction. WhatsApp + Notion is planned for the UK market — same architecture, different channel.
jobagent.decision.1.why: Tested multiple models. A slow tool breaks the workflow — if analysis takes 2 minutes, you lose the moment of decision. Optimised for latency first, not the highest possible output quality.
jobagent.decision.2.why: A cover letter alone solves the writing problem but not the fit problem. Match scoring and interview prep address the full arc — from 'should I apply?' to 'am I ready for the call?'
jobagent.reflection.label: What I'd Do Differently
jobagent.reflection.body: I built this for myself first — which meant one user and no feedback loop. The next version needs real user sessions. I don't yet know whether the interview prep output is what someone actually wants the night before an interview, or whether it surfaces the wrong kind of anxiety. The skill gap scoring is also only calibrated against my own profile — I don't know how well it generalises.
jobagent.arch.label: Workflow
jobagent.step.0.label: Input
jobagent.step.0.sub: URL, raw text, or Feishu message
jobagent.step.1.label: Parse & Fetch
jobagent.step.2.label: Analyse
jobagent.step.3.label: Output
jobagent.feature.0.title: Skill match scoring
jobagent.feature.0.body: Compares your candidate profile against the parsed JD, returning a 0–100 match score with a per-skill breakdown table showing met, partial, and missing requirements.
jobagent.feature.1.title: Cover letter generation
jobagent.feature.1.body: Selects the best-fit template from 5 role-specific options using keyword frequency, then generates a tailored cover letter that highlights relevant experience and addresses skill gaps.
jobagent.feature.2.title: ATS keyword optimisation
jobagent.feature.2.body: Extracts high-value keywords from the JD and recommends which ones to weave into your resume and cover letter to maximise applicant tracking system pass-through.
jobagent.feature.3.title: Feishu chatbot interface
jobagent.feature.3.body: Send a job URL in Feishu and get the full analysis back as a chat message. Background threading handles the 3-second webhook timeout while the analysis runs.
jobagent.footer: Source code, setup guide, and sample outputs on GitHub.
project.olist-churn.title: Olist Customer Intelligence & Churn Prevention
project.olist-churn.insight: 97% of customers buy only once — an end-to-end ML pipeline to predict who's about to leave and quantify the cost of letting them go.
project.olist-churn.problem: Olist, Brazil's largest e-commerce marketplace, faces a severe retention crisis: over 97% of customers make a single purchase and never return, leaving substantial lifetime value unrealised. Acquisition-focused metrics masked this structural weakness entirely.
project.olist-churn.method: Built a full customer analytics pipeline on 100K+ transactions across 2016–2018. Steps: RFM segmentation with quintile scoring, K-Means clustering (silhouette-validated, k=4), cohort retention heatmaps, CLV modelling, and a Random Forest churn classifier trained on recency, frequency, monetary value, and delivery experience features. Paired with an interactive A/B test sample size calculator for experiment planning.
project.olist-churn.finding: Recency is the dominant churn predictor. K-Means surfaces 4 behaviorally distinct segments that sharpen the RFM rules. Top 20% of customers drive ~80% of revenue — yet most remain single-purchase buyers with untapped repeat potential. The Random Forest model achieves strong ROC-AUC, identifying at-risk customers before they silently lapse.
project.olist-churn.impact: Targeted retention of high-value at-risk segments projects a 3–5× ROI on campaign spend. The accompanying A/B test calculator operationalises the findings — letting the team size experiments rigorously before launch, turning a one-off analysis into a repeatable retention infrastructure.
